content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 20, 1994


J. Scott Zundel
73-255 El Paseo, Suite 19
Palm Desert, CA 92260

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-478a

Dear Mr. Zundel:

This is in response to your letter requesting clarification
of advice provided to you on behalf of Councilmember Sybil Jaffy
(Advice Letter No. A-93-478) with respect to her responsibilities
as a Rancho Mirage City Councilmember pursuant to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

In your letter requesting clarification, you stated that you
have been informed that Councilmember Jaffy does not have an
ownership interest in any property of Desert Villas, except for
her ownership interest in her unit.

As you are aware, Commission advice is based on the facts
presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)
It appeared from the material you provided that in fact the
councilmember's unit was a condominium. We note, for example,
that the appraisal you submitted with your request indicated the
complex was a condominium complex. (See "project improvements"
section, appraisal headings, and "Supplemental Addendum Sheet," p.
2.)

As we stated in our letter, generally, ownership of a
condominium is considered an undivided interest in common in a
portion of real property coupled with a separate interest in a
space called a unit. (Jones Advice Letter, No. A-90-715; Civil


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Sections,
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-478a
Page 2


Code Section 1351(f).) Thus, it appears that the analysis in the
initial response is accurate.²

However, to the extent that the appraisal was inaccurate,³
and the councilmember's unit is in a complex is not a condominium complex but a
planned unit development in which the councilmember does not have
an undivided interest in the common area of the complex, the
standards to determine materiality would be different from those
set forth in the prior letter. Since the councilmember's unit is
more than 300 feet from the project site, Regulation 18702.3(a)(3)
provides that the effect of the decisions is material if:

[T]he decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.

Of course, since in the prior letter we concluded that the
"public generally" exception would permit participation, this
different standard would not affect the conclusion of that letter.

I hope this clarifies the application of the Act to your
situation. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


2 In addition, please note that even planned unit developments
are considered "common interest developments." (Civil Code
Section 1351(c)(3).) "Planned developments" may have a common
area which is owned by an association or in common by the owners
of the separate interests. (Civil Code Section 1351(k).)

3 As we stated in our prior letter, the Commission cannot
evaluate the accuracy of the appraisals. You have indicated
that the appraisal may be inaccurate as to the type of complex in which
the councilmember lives. Please be aware that formal written
advice is the application of the law to a particular set of facts
provided by the requestor. Any immunity that flows from the
letter is only applicable to the extent that the underlying facts
that you have submitted are accurate.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember Sybil Jaffy's ownership interest in a condominium unit at Desert Villas creates a conflict of interest requiring her recusal from decisions affecting the property, and whether the materiality standards differ if the complex is a planned unit development rather than a condominium.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the prior advice letter's analysis was accurate based on the condominium ownership structure. However, if the complex is actually a planned unit development where the councilmember has no undivided interest in common areas, the materiality standards would be different under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), requiring a $10,000 or more effect on fair market value or $1,000 or more annual rental value impact. The "public generally" exception would still likely permit participation even under this alternative standard.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a follow-up clarification to a prior advice letter (A-93-478). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers are present; question and conclusion extracted synthesized from body text. Document contains substantive discussion of condominium vs. planned unit development analysis and materiality standards. Minor OCR artifacts present in original text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Councilmember Sybil Jaffy's ownership interest in a condominium unit at Desert Villas creates a conflict of interest requiring her recusal from decisions affecting the property, and whether the materiality standards differ if the complex is a planned unit development rather than a condominium.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that the prior advice letter's analysis was accurate based on the condominium ownership structure. However, if the complex is actually a planned unit development where the councilmember has no undivided interest in common areas, the materiality standards would be different under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), requiring a $10,000 or more effect on fair market value or $1,000 or more annual rental value impact. The "public generally" exception would still likely permit participation even under this alternative standard.

embedding.summary: This is a clarification letter responding to questions about a prior advice letter (A-93-478) regarding Councilmember Jaffy's conflict of interest obligations related to her Desert Villas condominium unit ownership. The FPPC reaffirms its original analysis while noting that different materiality standards would apply if the property is actually a planned unit development rather than a condominium.
